hero.title: 🧠 Physiology
hero.subtitle: The intuitive mind is a sacred gift and the rational mind is a faithful servant - Albert Einstein

sections.hint: Focus on lesion localization, gaze pathways, brainstem syndromes, and basal ganglia circuits.

topics.content:
• Endocrine vs Exocrine: Endocrine = hormones into blood (distant effect); Exocrine = ducts → local action (enzymes, sweat).
  • Hormone Types: Peptides (water-soluble), Steroids (cholesterol-derived, lipid-soluble), Amines (mixed behavior).
  • Water-Soluble Hormones: Stored in vesicles, circulate free, bind cell surface receptors → act via 2nd messengers (FAST).
  • Lipid-Soluble Hormones: Synthesized on demand, travel bound to proteins, bind intracellular receptors → alter gene transcription (SLOW).
  • Peptide Hormone Synthesis: Preprohormone → Prohormone (ER/Golgi) → Stored in vesicles → Released by exocytosis.
  • Steroid Hormones: Derived from cholesterol (4-ring structure) → diffuse across membranes → no storage.
  • Transport: Water-soluble = free in plasma; Steroids/thyroid = bound to carrier proteins (inactive reservoir, longer half-life).
  • Half-Life: Peptides = short (minutes); Steroids/thyroid = long (hours–days).
  • Receptors: Water-soluble = membrane receptors; Lipid-soluble = intracellular (cytoplasm/nucleus).
  • Negative Feedback: Free hormone levels regulate secretion → maintains homeostasis.
  • Major Glands: Hypothalamus, Pituitary (master), Thyroid, Parathyroid, Adrenal, Pancreas, Gonads.
  • Functions: Regulates metabolism, growth, reproduction, blood pressure, electrolytes, behavior.

ers_physio1.q: Which of the following best describes an endocrine gland?
a: Secretes substances through ducts to a surface
Releases hormones directly into the bloodstream
Acts only locally at site of release
ers_physio1.l: Endocrine glands release hormones directly into the bloodstream to act on distant targets.

ers_physio1.q: Which of the following is an example of an exocrine function of the pancreas?
ers_physio1.l: The exocrine pancreas secretes digestive enzymes into ducts leading to the intestine.

ers_physio1.q: Which hormone type is most likely stored in secretory vesicles?
ers_physio1.l: Water-soluble peptide hormones are synthesized and stored in vesicles for rapid release.

ers_physio1.q: Which hormone type is synthesized on demand rather than stored?
ers_physio1.l: Steroid hormones are lipid-soluble and synthesized when needed from cholesterol.

ers_physio1.q: Which of the following is TRUE about water-soluble hormones?
ers_physio1.l: Water-soluble hormones bind surface receptors and act via second messenger systems.

ers_physio1.q: Which of the following is TRUE about lipid-soluble hormones?
a: They act via cell surface receptors
ers_physio1.l: Lipid-soluble hormones enter cells and alter gene transcription, leading to protein synthesis.

ers_physio1.q: Which hormone class includes thyroid hormones?
ers_physio1.l: Thyroid hormones are amines but behave like lipid-soluble hormones.

ers_physio1.q: Where are peptide hormones synthesized?
ers_physio1.l: Peptide hormones are synthesized in the rough ER as preprohormones.

ers_physio1.q: What is the first step in peptide hormone synthesis?
ers_physio1.l: Peptide hormones begin as preprohormones before being processed into prohormones.

ers_physio1.q: Which organelle converts preprohormone to prohormone?
ers_physio1.l: The ER cleaves preprohormone into prohormone.

ers_physio1.q: Where is prohormone packaged into vesicles?
ers_physio1.l: The Golgi apparatus packages prohormones into secretory vesicles.

ers_physio1.q: What triggers peptide hormone release?
ers_physio1.l: Peptide hormones are released via exocytosis when vesicles fuse with the membrane.

ers_physio1.q: Which of the following hormones is derived from cholesterol?
ers_physio1.l: Steroid hormones like cortisol are synthesized from cholesterol.

ers_physio1.q: Which gland produces cortisol?
ers_physio1.l: Cortisol is produced by the adrenal cortex.

ers_physio1.q: Which structure is part of steroid hormone backbone?
ers_physio1.l: Steroid hormones share a four-ring cholesterol backbone.

ers_physio1.q: Which hormone travels mostly bound in plasma?
ers_physio1.l: Thyroid hormone is mostly bound to carrier proteins in blood.

ers_physio1.q: What is the function of carrier proteins?
ers_physio1.l: Carrier proteins increase hormone stability and prolong half-life.

ers_physio1.q: Which hormone has the shortest half-life?
ers_physio1.l: Peptide hormones have short half-lives (minutes).

ers_physio1.q: Where do lipid-soluble hormones bind receptors?
ers_physio1.l: Lipid-soluble hormones bind intracellular receptors.

ers_physio1.q: Which system works with endocrine system to maintain homeostasis?
ers_physio1.l: Endocrine and nervous systems coordinate to regulate homeostasis.

ers_physio1.q: Which of the following is regulated by hormones?
ers_physio1.l: Hormones regulate metabolism, reproduction, blood pressure, and more.

ers_physio1.q: Which gland is considered the master endocrine gland?
ers_physio1.l: The pituitary gland controls other endocrine glands.

ers_physio1.q: Which hormone is produced in the pancreas?
ers_physio1.l: The pancreas produces insulin from beta cells.

ers_physio1.q: What is C-peptide?
ers_physio1.l: C-peptide is released when proinsulin is converted into insulin.

ers_physio1.q: What maintains hormone levels in the body?
ers_physio1.l: Negative feedback regulates hormone levels to maintain balance.

ers_physio1.q: Which form of hormone provides negative feedback?
ers_physio1.l: The free (active) hormone level controls feedback mechanisms.

ers_physio1.q: Which of the following describes hormone action speed?
ers_physio1.l: Peptides act fast via second messengers; steroids act slowly via gene transcription.

ers_physio1.q: Which gland produces aldosterone?
ers_physio1.l: Aldosterone is a steroid hormone produced by the adrenal cortex.

ers_physio1.q: Which of the following hormones can diffuse through cell membranes?
ers_physio1.l: Steroid hormones are lipid-soluble and diffuse through membranes.

ers_physio1.q: Which of the following best describes endocrine signaling?
ers_physio1.l: Endocrine hormones travel through blood to distant target organs.
